title: Margin, Metric & Distribution Losses
description: Flashcards for margin losses, KL divergence, ranking losses, hinge/cosine embedding losses, and triplet metric learning.

cards.front: **SoftMarginLoss** ‚Äî what label convention does it use?
cards.back:
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \log(1 + e^{-y_i \cdot z_i})$$

Labels $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ (not 0/1). Input $z_i$ is a raw score (logit).

This is binary logistic regression with the $\pm 1$ convention.

*PyTorch: `nn.SoftMarginLoss()` ¬∑ equivalent to sigmoid cross-entropy with {-1,+1} labels*

cards.front: **MultiLabelSoftMarginLoss** ‚Äî how does it differ from SoftMarginLoss?
cards.back:
Applies the same soft-margin loss independently to each of $C$ output classes:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{C}\sum_j \left[y_j \log\sigma(z_j) + (1-y_j)\log(1-\sigma(z_j))\right]$$

Target shape $(B, C)$ with values $\in\{0,1\}$.

*Use when: each sample can belong to multiple classes simultaneously*

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \max(0,\; 1 - z_{y_i} + z_j)^p \text{ for } j \neq y_i$$

Penalises any class $j \neq y_i$ whose score comes within margin 1 of the correct class score.

*Multi-class SVM hinge loss. PyTorch: `nn.MultiMarginLoss(p=1, margin=1.0)`*

**KLDivLoss** (Kullback-Leibler Divergence)

Measures how much distribution $P$ (target) differs from $Q$ (model). **Asymmetric** ‚Äî $D_{KL}(P\|Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q\|P)$.

**Warning:** PyTorch `KLDivLoss` expects **log-probabilities** as input, not raw probabilities.

*Use when: distilling soft labels; VAE ELBO; teacher-student training*

$$\mathcal{L} = \max(0,\; -y(z_1 - z_2) + \text{margin})$$

$y = +1$ means $z_1$ should rank higher than $z_2$; $y = -1$ means the opposite.

Loss is zero when the correct item ranks above the incorrect one by at least `margin`.

*Use when: ranking documents, search results, or recommendation items pairwise*

$$\mathcal{L}_i = \begin{cases} d_i & y_i = 1 \\ \max(0,\; \text{margin} - d_i) & y_i = -1 \end{cases}$$

$d_i = \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{y}_i\|$ is a pre-computed distance. Labels $y_i \in \{1, -1\}$.

*Pulls similar pairs together; pushes dissimilar pairs apart. Input is already a distance ‚Äî not embeddings.*

cards.front: **CosineEmbeddingLoss** ‚Äî formula and when to use it
cards.back:
$$\mathcal{L}_i = \begin{cases} 1 - \cos(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2) & y_i = 1 \\ \max(0,\; \cos(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2) - \text{margin}) & y_i = -1 \end{cases}$$

Optimises **angular similarity** rather than Euclidean distance.

*Use when: direction matters more than magnitude ‚Äî NLP sentence similarity, face verification*

$$d(a, p) + \alpha < d(a, n)$$

The anchor-positive distance must be smaller than the anchor-negative distance by at least margin $\alpha > 0$.

*Anchor $a$, positive $p$ (same class), negative $n$ (different class)*

**TripletMarginLoss**

Zero when the constraint is satisfied by margin $\alpha$; positive otherwise.

*PyTorch: `nn.TripletMarginLoss(margin=1.0, p=2)` ¬∑ default $\ell^2$ distance*

*Use when: learning an embedding space where class membership defines similarity*

Accepts a **callable distance function** instead of requiring $\ell^p$ distance:

```python
nn.TripletMarginWithDistanceLoss(
    distance_function=lambda a, b: 1 - F.cosine_similarity(a, b)
)
```

*Use when: cosine distance or a learned metric is more appropriate than Euclidean distance*

**Easy negative:** $d(a,n) > d(a,p) + \alpha$ ‚Äî already satisfied, contributes zero loss.
**Hard negative:** $d(a,n) < d(a,p)$ ‚Äî most violated; risk of model collapse.
**Semi-hard:** $d(a,p) < d(a,n) < d(a,p) + \alpha$ ‚Äî violated but not inverted; most informative.

*Random sampling is dominated by easy negatives. Online semi-hard mining (FaceNet) trains ~10√ó faster.*

| Loss | Negatives per update | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Triplet | 1 | explicit (a, p, n) triplets |
| Contrastive | 1 | pairs; margin on distance |
| NT-Xent (SimCLR) | $2(B-1)$ | all other batch items as negatives |

*NT-Xent scales better with batch size ‚Äî more signal per forward pass. Triplet needs careful mining.*
